title: 17 Questions About ‘Mortal Kombat’ and the Worst Video Game Movies Ever
format: Sean Fennessey brings in Ringer writers and gamers Justin Charity and Ben Lindbergh to dig into the new Mortal Kombat, then work through why video game movies are so reliably bad. Each one names a title they'd actually defend.

films.title: Mortal Kombat (2021)
films.note: Simon McQuoid's reboot. It cuts Johnny Cage and the tournament, adds an original POV lead (Cole Young), and hangs everything on Arcana lore. Better production than the '95 film but, they agree, not campy enough to be a cult object. Kano is the runaway MVP.

films.title: Mortal Kombat (1995)
films.note: Paul W.S. Anderson's original. All three revisited it; nostalgia aside, none thought it held up. Sean didn't find it campy in the good way.

films.title: Mortal Kombat: Annihilation
films.note: The sequel Ben cites as proof that stringing fights together with no story doesn't work either.

segments.heading: The video game movie curse
segments.note: why the track record is so bad

films.title: Super Mario Bros.
films.note: Where Ben says the curse starts: a movie bearing almost no resemblance to the game, despite Bob Hoskins and Dennis Hopper. Justin recaps its plot in bewildered second person.

films.title: Lara Croft: Tomb Raider
films.note: The Angelina Jolie run, cited among the early platformer-and-fighting-game adaptations.

films.title: Tomb Raider
films.note: Ben's "canon of competence" alongside Sonic and Detective Pikachu.

films.title: Assassin's Creed
films.note: Justin Kurzel off an acclaimed Macbeth, Fassbender starring, and still, Ben says, "hot nonsense." A studio-scale flop.

films.title: Prince of Persia: The Sands of Time
films.note: Mike Newell directing; Sean marks it as the film that reset Jake Gyllenhaal's career for a decade.

films.title: Ready Player One
films.note: Not a video game adaptation but of this world: Spielberg's fan-service machine that, Sean notes, has basically vanished from the conversation.

films.title: Boss Level
films.note: Ben's example of a movie that copies video game logic (a time loop with Frank Grillo) better than most actual adaptations do.

films.title: Scott Pilgrim vs. the World
films.note: Cited among films that borrow gaming's look and conceit rather than adapt a specific title.

films.title: Edge of Tomorrow
films.note: The action-movie respawn loop: video game DNA without a game license.

films.title: Sonic the Hedgehog
films.note: One of the "credible, likable kids movie" adaptations Justin argues fans don't count when tallying the curse.

films.title: Street Fighter II: The Animated Movie
films.note: Justin's case that anime adaptations translate the fighting-game experience better than live action does.

films.title: Monster Hunter
films.note: Paul W.S. Anderson's most recent adaptation; Sean saw it, the guests hadn't, so it's tabled for next time.

films.title: Colette
films.note: Ben's "a video game movie won an Oscar" aside: the documentary short bundled with the Medal of Honor: Above and Beyond VR game, which took Best Documentary Short the night before.

picks.title: Pokémon Detective Pikachu
picks.note: Actually laugh-out-loud funny, uses the source loosely, and there's a real thrill in seeing the world rendered at blockbuster budget. Ryan Reynolds in Pikachu's body carries it.

picks.title: Resident Evil: Afterlife
picks.note: Paul W.S. Anderson, 2010. Not faithful to the games' claustrophobic horror, but in touch with their VHS-schlock spirit; Justin's fascinated by the Ouroboros of games and movies feeding each other.

picks.title: Silent Hill
picks.note: Sean's near-lone recommendation: genuinely scary, stands on its own, oddly left out of these conversations. Radha Mitchell, Sean Bean.

TV: The Falcon and the Winter Soldier
